content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 907 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance: Administration        Executive Legal        Enforcement        Conflict of Interest
916 327 5460             322 5901            322 4441            222 0444

April 12, 1979

Senator Robert Nimmo
State Capitol, Room 3070
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Senator Nimmo:

     Thank you for sending me a copy of a letter from Monterey
County Supervisor Barbara Shipnuck.  Her letter raises several
points which deserve comment.

     The Political Reform Act requires all officials whose
decisions affect economic interests to file Statements of
Economic Interests pursuant to Conflicts of Interest Codes
adopted by the agencies.  A person appointed to one of these
agencies must file an appropriate statement within 10 days after
appointment.

     Apparently, Ms. Shipnuck was mistakenly informed that her
statement was due 10 days after her appointment.  Because she
was misinformed, her fine has been waived by agency.

     It should be noted that Ms. Shipnuck, like many local
officials, serves on a number of county and regional boards.
Each of these boards has a different code, with different filing
requirements and perhaps different filing dates.

     The Political Reform Act, unlike the Unruh Act passed by the
legislature in 1969, does not require broad disclosure of all
interests of a public official.  Rather, it requires that each
agency examine what economic interests its officials can affect
and only these interests are disclosed by the officials.  Thus,
Ms. Shipnuck does not have to disclose real property located in
Los Angeles County because she cannot make a decision as a
Monterey County Supervisor to affect that property.  As a super-
visor she need only disclose investments of entities that do
business in Monterey County and interests in real property that
are in the county.

     It is my understanding that the Air Pollution Control Dis-
trict has jurisdiction over more than just the county so that the
district's code will require additional disclosure of interests
outside Monterey County but within the district.

Senator Robert Nimmo
Page Two                                April 12, 1979


     Because each entity must have a code tailored to its juris-
diction and its powers, Ms. Shipnuck probably will have different
filing requirements. A possible solution for Ms. Shipnuck is to
combine the requirements of each of the entities so that she only
has to complete one statement which can be sent to all her agencies.

     Her second point discusses when statements are filed. She is
right in stating that statements are filed at different times.
Each agency is given discretion to determine when the statements
are due. We think it is better to give the agencies local control
to determine when and what is filed rather than imposing a uniform
date.

     Finally, she indicates that she should only file one statement
in one place. We believe that each agency should have a copy of
her statement on file with the agency. Since the statements can
be identical in each case, we do not believe that the three filings
will be a great burden on her nor do we believe it will be a bur-
den for each agency to review her statement.

     Please feel free to call on me if you have any questions or
comments.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [illegible]
                                        Robert M. Stern
                                        General Counsel

cc: Barbara Shipnuck

1300 CLOSURE, SUITE 2-B
SAN LUIS OBISPO 93401
(805) 544-2274

2555 GARDEN, SUITE 304
MONTEREY 93940
(408) 649-2932

701 OCEAN, ROOM 540
SANTA CRUZ 95060
(408) 429-2111

California State Senate

COMMITTEES
RULES
PRISONS
AGRICULTURE AND
WATER RESOURCES

ROBERT P. NIMMO
SENATOR
SANTA CRUZ, MONTEREY, SAN LUIS OBISPO COUNTIES,
AND A PORTION OF SANTA BARBARA COUNTY

March 26, 1979

Mr. Robert Stern
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Bob:

Enclosed is a copy of a letter I have received
from Monterey County Supervisor, Barbara Shipnuck requesting
the introduction of legislation to streamline the filing
procedures used by your Commission. I believe you will
find her letter self-explanatory.

I would very much appreciate receiving your
comments on her suggested legislation.

As always, your assistance is very much appreciated.

Kindest regards.

Sincerely,

Bob Nimmo

ROBERT P. NIMMO

RPN:lbD

Enclosure

MONTEREY COUNTY

THE BOARD OF SUPERVISORS

THE COURTHOUSE - 240 CHURCH STREET - P.O. BOX 1004 - SALINAS, CA. 93902

BARBARA SHIPNUCK
SUPERVISOR - DISTRICT 2

KALAH BUMBA
ADMINISTRATIVE ASSISTANT

424-8611, EXT. 498                                    March 19, 1979




The Honorable Robert P. Nimmo
17th District, State Senate
State Capitol
Sacramento, Ca. 95814


Dear Senator Nimmo:

     I have enclosed a letter I wrote to the Fair Political Prac-
tice Commission. I believe it's contents are self-explanatory.
I am convinced that legislation streamlining the filing pro-
cedures used by the Fair Political Practice Commission would
be in the public interest. We must cut down unnecessary
bureaucracy at the state level as well as at the local level.




                                             Sincerely,


                                             [signature]
                                             Barbara Shipnuck




BS:krb

MONTEREY COUNTY

THE BOARD OF SUPERVISORS

THE COURTHOUSE - 240 CHURCH STREET - P.O. BOX 1004 - SALINAS, CA. 93902

BARBARA SHIPNUCK
SUPERVISOR - DISTRICT 2

KALAH BUMBA
ADMINISTRATIVE ASSISTANT

424-8611, EXT. 488                                March 15, 1979


Alice Hughes, Documents Examiner                    Certified Mail-Return
State of California                                 Receipt Requested
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, Ca. 95804

Dear Ms. Hughes:

Your letter of March 1st asks that I furnish your agency with a reason
that justifies the late filing on my Statement of Economic Interests.
According to the regulation under which I was proceeding, my Statement
of Economic Interest was due on February 8th, thirty days after my ap-
pointment to the Air Pollution Control District Board, as a representa-
tive of the Monterey County Board of Supervisors and not January 18,
nine days after my appointment. I, therefore, admit of no error in my
filing which in fact was ten days early, not, as you claim, eleven days
late, and I have no intention of remitting a fine to your agency.

This experience suggests to me the need for legislation bringing all
filing dates into conformity, and requiring one elected official to
file only one statement which will then be valid for all agencies to
which he or she is appointed to serve as a result of his/her elected
status.

According to your calculations I should have filed one form 10 days after
being seated on the Board of Supervisors and another 20 days later. I have
since filed a third form on March 1 for our Sanitation District. This
triplication of my effort is a waste of taxpayers' money as it takes 3
times the staff to read and process three forms filled out by one elected
official as it would take to read and process it one form and process it.

Thank you for so clearly bringing this to my attention. I intend to take
the matter up with my legislators.


Sincerely,

Barbara Shipnuck

cc:Assemblywoman Carol Hallett
   Assemblyman Henry Mello
   Senator Robert Nimmo

The Political Reform Act requires all officials whose decisions affect economic interests to file Statements of Economic Interests pursuant to Conflicts of Interest Codes adopted by their agencies. A person appointed to one of these agencies must file an appropriate statement within 10 days after appointment. Apparently, Ms. Shipnuck was mistakenly informed that her statement was due 30 days after her appointment. Because she was misinformed, her fine has been waived by our agency. It should be noted that Ms. Shipnuck, like many local officials, serves on a number of county and regional boards. Each of these boards has a different code, with different filing requirements and perhaps different filing dates. The Political Reform Act, unlike the Unruh Act passed by the legislature in 1969, does not require broad disclosure of all interests of a public official. Rather, it requires that each agency examine what economic interests its officials can affect and only these interests are disclosed by the officials. Thus, Ms. Shipnuck does not have to disclose real property located in Los Angeles County because she cannot make a decision as a Monterey County Supervisor to affect that property. As a supervisor she need only disclose investments of entities that do business in Monterey County and interests in real property that are in the county. It is my understanding that the Air Pollution Control District has jurisdiction over more than just the county so that the district's code will require additional disclosure of interests outside Monterey County but within the district.
